content.decision_question: Should Hungary adopt formal procedural safeguards (alternatives assessment, reciprocal contracts) within the existing centralized authority structure, or should it first restore genuine municipal/local bargaining power and legal standing as a precondition for such safeguards to be substantive rather than symbolic?
content.evidence_boundary: Evidence can clarify whether the 2026 reform vehicle creates enforceable rights versus discretionary consultation, and can document historical patterns of informal-only reversal (F-live-legal_and_governance-08). It cannot resolve the underlying political choice of whether central authorities are willing to cede real decision power, which is a governance-design and value choice, not an empirical question.
content.tension: Adding a presumption-against-closure duty (T1) or reciprocal contracts (T2) can force documented consideration of alternatives, but both leave the core closure decision with the same centralized tankerületi/state authority that currently applies enrollment thresholds, risking a purely symbolic safeguard unless paired with real veto power or restored municipal standing.
content.title: Procedural safeguard versus genuine authority transfer
value_poles: administrative simplicity and centralized cost control
